title: Lathyrus grandifolius
taxonomy: Lathyrus grandifolius is classified as a species of flowering plant in the genus Lathyrus, which is a part of the family Fabaceae. It belongs to the order Fabales and is a member of the subfamily Faboideae. The exact classification is as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Clade: Angiosperms, Clade: Eudicots, Clade: Rosids, Order: Fabales, Family: Fabaceae, Genus: Lathyrus, Species: L. grandifolius. The classification reflects its evolutionary relationships and characteristics. Lathyrus grandifolius is a part of the legume family, which is known for its diverse range of plants.
uses: Lathyrus grandifolius, commonly known as the broad-leaved pea, has been used in traditional medicine. Its seeds contain a protein called ODAP, which has been used to treat certain medical conditions. However, excessive consumption of ODAP can be toxic and cause neurolathyrism, a condition affecting the nervous system. In some cultures, the plant is used to treat various ailments, including fever and digestive issues. More research is needed to fully understand the medicinal properties and potential risks associated with using Lathyrus grandifolius.

side_effects: Lathyrus grandifolius, a plant native to certain regions, contains a toxin called ODAP (oxalyldiaminopropionic acid) in its seeds, which can cause neurological and other health issues if ingested in large quantities. If consumed in significant amounts over a prolonged period, it can lead to neurolathyrism, a condition characterized by muscle spasms, paralysis, and other severe symptoms. The toxin can cause irreversible damage to the nervous system and is particularly hazardous for pregnant women and young children. Consuming the seeds can also lead to kidney damage and other complications. It's essential to avoid eating the seeds of Lathyrus grandifolius altogether.
intro: Lathyrus grandifolius, commonly known as the large-leafed peacock's ear or grass pea, is a perennial legume plant native to the mountainous regions of Central Asia. The plant is rich in nutritional value and has been used in traditional medicine to provide health benefits, including its potential to lower cholesterol levels and reduce inflammation. The bioactive constituents of Lathyrus grandifolius include alkaloids, flavonoids, saponins, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Lathyrus grandifolius is often used to make herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and capsules, which are traditionally used to support cardiovascular health and alleviate symptoms of digestive disorders.
